description: You are a psychology professor at CMU preparing a visual search experiment based on Treisman & Gelade (1980) for your Perception & Attention course lab. A task specification file has been placed on the Desktop at ~/Desktop/visual_search_spec.txt — read it carefully, as it defines all required parameters. Your job: (1) Read the specification file to understand the exact experimental design. (2) Write a complete, runnable PEBL script implementing the specified visual search task and save it to ~/pebl/tasks/visual_search/visual_search_task.pbl. (3) Run the task once with subject ID 'pilot01' using 'run-pebl ~/pebl/tasks/visual_search/visual_search_task.pbl' to confirm it executes without crashing — accept defaults or press keys as needed during the practice phase, then quit early by pressing Escape or Q when prompted. (4) Write a brief design rationale document to ~/pebl/tasks/visual_search/design_rationale.txt explaining how your implementation maps to the specification. The PEBL script must implement the parameters in the specification exactly: correct timing values, required search conditions (feature and conjunction), required set sizes, and correct response keys.

metadata.paradigm: Treisman & Gelade (1980) feature vs conjunction search
metadata.reference: Treisman, A.M. & Gelade, G. (1980). A feature-integration theory of attention. Cogn Psychol, 12(1):97-136.
